title: On the Architecture of Lies
author: From: Mark Twain
content: A lie can travel halfway around the world while the truth is putting on its shoes. In a project, slow-moving truth is more valuable than fast-moving rumors.
action: Action: Today, prioritize 'brutal transparency' in a status report, even if the facts are inconvenient.

title: The Stockdale Paradox
author: From: Jim Collins (Good to Great)
content: You must maintain unwavering faith that you will prevail in the end, regardless of the difficulties, AND at the same time have the discipline to confront the most brutal facts of your current reality.
action: Action: Write down the 'brutal fact' currently threatening your project and a counter-strategy for it.

title: The Map-Territory Relation
author: From: Alfred Korzybski
content: The map is not the territory. Your project plan is just a representation; it is not the actual work. Do not fall in love with the documentation.
action: Action: Spend 15 minutes engaging with the actual 'work' (the code, the soil, the logs) rather than managing the plan.

title: The Zeigarnik Effect
author: From: Bluma Zeigarnik
content: Our brains remember uncompleted tasks better than completed ones. This creates a 'tension' that drains energy until the task is closed.
action: Action: Identify one 'open loop' that is nagging at you and either finish it or formally schedule it for a later date.

title: The Halo Effect
author: From: Edward Thorndike
content: If you like one trait of a person or project, you assume all their other traits are good. This blinds you to specific flaws.
action: Action: Pick one project you love and perform a 'stress test' to find its single weakest component.
